title: Small Stock Dividend
event: Reyes Co. declares a 5% stock dividend on its 100,000 outstanding shares of $1 par stock when the market price is $12 per share. Prepare the declaration entry.

lines.label: 5,000 shares × $12 market
lines.why: Small stock dividends (< 20–25%) capitalize retained earnings at MARKET value.
lines.trap: Market for small dividends, PAR for large ones — the size of the dividend picks the price.

lines.label: 5,000 × $1 par
lines.why: Par value of the shares to be issued — an EQUITY account, not a liability.
lines.trap: 'Distributable,' not 'Payable' — no assets will ever leave, so it's not debt.

lines.account: Paid-in Capital in Excess of Par
lines.label: 5,000 × $11
lines.why: The excess of market over par lands in PIC, as with any issuance.
lines.trap: Total equity is UNCHANGED — the entry just shuffles amounts between equity accounts.

memorize.body: A stock dividend moves equity between its own pockets: RE down, contributed capital up, total equity identical. Shareholders' percentage ownership is also unchanged.
Small (< 20–25%) → market value. Large → par value. Stock SPLITS get no entry at all — only a memo.

questions.prompt: 5% stock dividend, 100,000 shares outstanding, $1 par, $12 market. Retained earnings debited = ?

distractors.feedback: That's par value — the LARGE-dividend treatment. Small dividends capitalize at MARKET: 5,000 × $12.
That's a stock SPLIT. A stock dividend requires an entry — RE is capitalized at market for small dividends.

accountBank: Paid-in Capital in Excess of Par
build.scaffold: Debit RE at market; credit par to Distributable; PIC takes the spread. Nothing here is a liability.
